summary: Four psalms of David spanning confession and forgiveness (Ps 32, 51), personal prayer in crisis (Ps 86), and joyful pilgrimage to the house of the LORD (Ps 122).
psalm_rows_html:
PsalmTypeAuthorSituationKey ThemeNotable VerseNT Connection
32Maskil (teaching)DavidAfter confession of sin (traditionally linked to the Bathsheba incident)Blessedness of forgiveness; the relief of honest confession before Godv.1 "Blessed is the one whose transgression is forgiven, whose sin is covered."Romans 4:7–8 (Paul quotes vv.1–2 to prove justification by faith apart from works)
51Penitential / MiktamDavidAfter Nathan confronted David about Bathsheba (2 Samuel 12)Repentance, cleansing, and renewal; original sin; the work of the Holy Spirit in restorationv.10 "Create in me a clean heart, O God, and renew a right spirit within me."Spirit regeneration (John 3:5; Ezekiel 36:26–27); v.4b quoted in Romans 3:4 to affirm divine righteousness
86Individual Lament / PrayerDavidPersonal crisis; surrounded by enemies and arrogant opponentsGod's unique goodness and steadfast love; prayer for mercy, teaching, and an undivided heartv.11 "Teach me your way, O LORD, that I may walk in your truth; unite my heart to fear your name."v.9 fulfilled in Revelation 15:4 (all nations coming to worship before God)
122Song of Ascents (Pilgrimage)DavidPilgrimage to Jerusalem for worship at the templeJoy in going to the house of the LORD; prayer for Jerusalem's peace and prosperityv.1 "I was glad when they said to me, 'Let us go to the house of the LORD!'"Points to the heavenly Jerusalem (Hebrews 12:22; Revelation 21:2–3)
